coredata.dc:title: Specific and visual assay of iodide ion in human urine via redox pretreatment using ratiometric fluorescent test paper printed with dimer DNA silver nanoclusters and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Panpan
Xu, Xin
Ji, Jiangrong
Wu, Jiafeng
Lu, Tian
Xia, Yuhong
Wang, Liping
Fan, Junting
Jin, Yang
Zhang, Liying
Du, Shuhu
coredata.dc:description: The fluorescence-based assay of iodide ion (I−) has been extensively studied by the use of different sensing probes and techniques, but it remains a tricky task to eliminate the interference of chloride ion (Cl−) for the analysis of low-level I− in complex genuine samples. Herein, we develop a redox pretreatment strategy for specific separating I− from human urine. Simultaneously, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe is constructed by a simple mixing of dimer DNA silver nanoclusters (dDNA-AgNCs) and carbon dots (CDs) with the ratio of 5:1 in fluorescent intensity, and used for visual assay of I−. After addition of I−, the fluorescence of orange dDNA-AgNCs can be quenched by I− as the result of I−-induced oxidative etching and aggregation of dDNA-AgNCs, while blue CDs as the stable internal standard are unresponsive to I−. With the increase of I−, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I577/I446) of binary-color probe gradually decreased, which leads to color variation from salmon pink to lighter salmon pink to lilac to light steel blue to final deep sky blue (under a UV lamp) with a sensitive detection limit of 19.8 nM. The assay for I− can also be convenient to implement for visual monitoring of I− by observing color change of test paper printed with the ratiometric probe, responding to 50 nM that is about 1 order of magnitude lower than the median urinary I− concentration defined by the World Health Organization (WHO) for school-age children. The sensitive test paper can provide an advanced platform for colorimetric and visual monitoring of I− in human urine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Redox pretreatment
Dimer DNA silver nanoclusters
Carbon dots
Binary-color probe ink
Fluorescent test paper
Iodide ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent measurement of iodide ion in aqueous solution
Visual monitoring of I− in human urine
Quenching mechanism of ratiometric fluorescent probe by iodide ion
Optimization of the ratiometric fluorescent method
Sensitivity and selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe to iodide ion
Visual assay of iodide ion in urine using ratiometric fluorescent test paper
Comparison of this work and other methods for assay of iodide ion

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic procedures of (A) dDNA-AgNCs and (B) CDs.

The fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 353 nm) of (a) dDNA-AgNCs, (b) CDs, and mixture of dDNA-AgNCs/CDs in the (c) absence and (d) presence of 9 μM I−. The corresponding inset photos were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.
Mechanism scheme for the recognition of I− by dDNA-AgNCs.

(A) The evolution of fluorescence spectra of the mixture of dDNA-AgNCs and CDs with the addition of I−. The inset photos show the corresponding color changes under a 365 nm UV lamp. (B) The calibration curve of fluorescence intensity ratio (I577/I446) of the binary-color probe vs I− concentration, and the fitted equation is y = − 0.3215 x + 4.8036, with R2 = 0.9985. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Ratiometric fluorescent responses to various negative ions (blue bars) and their mixture with I− ions (red bars). Blue bars: the selectivity experiments were done in phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4) with the addition of I− (9 μM) or other various negative ions (90 μM) into the mixture of dDNA-AgNCs and CDs (fluorescence intensity ratio, 5:1). The inset (a) shows the corresponding fluorescence color photos under a 365 nm UV lamp. Red bars: the anti-interferences capabilities were tested using the mixture of an excessive interfering ion (90 μM) with an I− (9 μM). The inset (b) shows the corresponding fluorescence color photos under a 365 nm UV lamp. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Visualization of I− using the fluorescent test papers prepared by printing dDNA-AgNCs/CDs ink on a piece of filter paper. (B) The recovery tests (n = 3) of I− in human urine. a–d: normal human urine spiked with 0, 1, 5, 9 μM I−, respectively. e–h: hyperthyroidism patient urine spiked with 0, 1, 5, 9 μM I−, respectively. Each sample was diluted 10-fold before measurement. The recovery of I− was determined by the linear relation of I577/I446 with the concentration of I−. (C) The corresponding test paper-based visual detection of above human urine samples. The photos were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.

(A) Feasibility of the pretreatment method. (a) probe, (b) probe + aqueous solution of I−, (c) probe + pretreated aqueous solution of I−, (d) probe + aqueous solution of Cl−, (e) probe + aqueous mixture of I− and Cl−, and (f) probe + pretreated aqueous mixture of I− and Cl− (I−: 9 μM; Cl−: 250 mM). (B) Fluorescent responses of probe to unpretreated urine and pretreated urine, respectively (urine: 10 μL).
Comparison of I− detection results in the previous and present works.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: “−” represents which had not been mentioned in the related literature.

head.ce:title: Specific and visual assay of iodide ion in human urine via redox pretreatment using ratiometric fluorescent test paper printed with dimer DNA silver nanoclusters and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence-based assay of iodide ion (I−) has been extensively studied by the use of different sensing probes and techniques, but it remains a tricky task to eliminate the interference of chloride ion (Cl−) for the analysis of low-level I− in complex genuine samples. Herein, we develop a redox pretreatment strategy for specific separating I− from human urine. Simultaneously, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe is constructed by a simple mixing of dimer DNA silver nanoclusters (dDNA-AgNCs) and carbon dots (CDs) with the ratio of 5:1 in fluorescent intensity, and used for visual assay of I−. After addition of I−, the fluorescence of orange dDNA-AgNCs can be quenched by I− as the result of I−-induced oxidative etching and aggregation of dDNA-AgNCs, while blue CDs as the stable internal standard are unresponsive to I−. With the increase of I−, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I577/I446) of binary-color probe gradually decreased, which leads to color variation from salmon pink to lighter salmon pink to lilac to light steel blue to final deep sky blue (under a UV lamp) with a sensitive detection limit of 19.8 nM. The assay for I− can also be convenient to implement for visual monitoring of I− by observing color change of test paper printed with the ratiometric probe, responding to 50 nM that is about 1 order of magnitude lower than the median urinary I− concentration defined by the World Health Organization (WHO) for school-age children. The sensitive test paper can provide an advanced platform for colorimetric and visual monitoring of I− in human urine.

ce:section.ce:para: Iodide ion (I−), as one of the essential trace elements to human body, is indispensable ingredient in the formation of thyroid hormones [triiodothyronine (T3) and thyroxine (T4)] that play important physiological roles in thyroid function, metabolism, growth and development, water-salt balance regulation and nervous activity [1,2]. The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends that daily iodine intakes for adult and pregnant woman are 150 μg and 250 μg, respectively [3]. The deficiency of iodine may cause spontaneous abortion, goiter, hypothyroidism, cretinism and mental retardation [4]. However, excessive intake of iodine also leads to goiter and hyperthyroidism [5]. It has been demonstrated that any excess iodine consumed by humans is excreted through urine in the form of I− [4], because the human organs cannot store iodine. Thus, in order to enable human to know their own iodine nutriture, the determination of I− in urine is very important.

Recently, the analytical methods for detection of I− mainly include ion chromatography [6], electrochemical analysis [7], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [8], high performance liquid chromatography coupled mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [9], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [10], and so on. Nevertheless, these methods cannot be used for convenient and instant detection of uric I− because of some of their demerits, such as sophisticated equipment, professional instrument operation, high testing cost, etc. To address the limitations of the above traditional analytical methods, researchers have developed various test membrane and test kit for real-time, on-site and cost-effective detection of uric I−, such as poly (N-vinyl-2-pyrrolidone)-stabilized gold nanoparticle (Au NPs)-based test membrane [4] and chitosan oligosaccharide lactate-capped silver nanoparticles (COL-AgNPs)-based test kit [5]. Although these two strategies realized convenient and colorimetric determination of I−, they only displayed the change of color brightness with I−, greatly limiting their practical capability. Especially, in their interference experiments of Cl−, the highest Cl− concentration used was only 825-fold higher than the concentration of I−. Actually, in normal human urine, the concentration of Cl− is about 120,000-fold higher than that of I− [11–13]. So, the specific detection of I− in human urine with ultrahigh concentration of Cl− is a great challenge. However, to the best of our knowledge, no method for the specific and visual detection of I− in human urine containing ultrahigh concentration of Cl− using test paper with wide color evolution is found in the previously reported literatures.

In this work, a new and portable platform for I− assay is developed by using a ratiometric fluorescent probe, which is constructed by dimer DNA silver nanoclusters (dDNA-AgNCs) and carbon dots (CDs). In the probe system, orange dDNA-AgNCs acted as the recognition unit for I−, and blue CDs served as the internal reference. As the increase of I−, the fluorescent intensity of orange dDNA-AgNCs was strongly quenched by I−, whereas that of blue CDs was hardly affected, accompanying successive fluorescent color evolutions. Given that, a convenient test paper was fabricated by inkjet printing the ratiometric fluorescent probe onto a piece of filter paper, and realized the specific and visual monitoring of I− in urine by a redox pretreatment, which can completely eliminate the interference of ultrahigh Cl− concentration. The results demonstrated that our platform has the potential to be used for the convenient I− analysis in human urine and diagnosis of diseases such as hyperthyroidism.

Potassium iodide (KI) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (Saint Louis, MO, USA). Ethylenediamine, citric acid, urea and carbon tetrachloride (CCl4) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), sodium thiosulfate (NaS2O3) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The DNA oligonucleotides used in this work were synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents applied in this research were analytical grade and without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) used in all experiments was prepared by a Millipore’s lab water purification system (Milfords, MA, USA).

The fluorescence spectra were recorded by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a xenon lamp and a quartz cuvette (optical length 10 mm) (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were determined by a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The size and morphology of nanomaterials were characterized by a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Jeol, Japan). The hydrodynamic sizes and zeta potential of nanomaterials were measured by the use of a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 particle size analyzer (Malvern, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a TENSOR 27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with a spectral range from 4000 to 400 cm−1 and a resolution of 2 cm−1. All fluorescent photos were taken under an AGL-9406 portable UV lamp (AGL, China) with a Nikon D3100 digital camera (Nikon, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime was analyzed by a Horiba FM-4P transient state fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) with excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Statistical analysis and data plotting were carried out in Origin Pro 2018 software (OriginLab, USA).

The dDNA-AgNCs were synthesized according to the previous literature with some modifications [14]. Briefly, 5 optical density (OD) (equal to 13.5 nmol) of DNA template was first dissolved in 880 μL of 20 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) containing 20 mM KNO3 to obtain single-stranded DNA solution. The solution was initially heated at 90 °C for 10 min, and then naturally cooled down to room temperature for at least 2 h to form dimer shaped DNA. Then, 10 μL of 8.1 mM freshly prepared AgNO3 solution was added to the mixture under the vortexed condition for 30 s. After incubation for 30 min at 25 °C, 10 μL of 8.1 mM freshly prepared NaBH4 was rapidly added into the mixture under vigorous stirring for 5 min, and incubated at 4 °C for 12 h in the dark to obtain dDNA-AgNCs. The final concentration of dDNA-AgNCs was 7.5 μM. For comparison, single-stranded DNA-AgNCs (sDNA-AgNCs) were also prepared by the method of previous literature [15]. See the experimental section and Fig. S1 in Electronic Supplementary Material (ESM).
CDs were prepared by the method of previous literature [16]. See the details of one step synthesis procedure in the experimental section of ESM.

In brief, a commercial ink cartridge was thoroughly washed with ultrapure water until the ink was completely cleared away to obtain a vacant cartridge, which was dried in an oven at 50 °C for 6 h. Three milliliters of mixing ink (2 mL of 7.5 μM dDNA-AgNCs solution and 1 mL of 50 ng/mL CDs solution) was injected into above vacant cartridge. A rectangle pattern (6 × 3 cm2) was printed on a filter paper through an inkjet printer connected to a computer, and the printing was re-done for 30 times. After air-drying for 10 min, the pattern was clipped into pieces of 3 × 1 cm2 to obtain test paper (for the colorimetric and visual assay of I−). The amounts of dDNA-AgNCs and CDs printed on the test paper are ∼0.83 nmol/cm2 and ∼2.78 ng/cm2, respectively, which are calculated using the concentrations of nanoparticles and area of filter paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent measurement of iodide ion in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Measurement of I− in aqueous solution: 30 μL of the binary-color probe solution (20 μL of dDNA-AgNCs and 10 μL of CDs) was first mixed with 60 μL of 20 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) containing 20 mM KNO3. Then, 10 μL of different concentrations of I− were added into the above mixing solution (final concentration of 0.05, 1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13 μM), and reacted for 1 min at 25 °C. Finally, the fluorescence intensities of the mixing system at 577 and 446 nm were measured with the excitation wavelength of 353 nm. Excitation and emission slits were all fixed at 10 nm for the fluorescence tests. All measurements were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual monitoring of I− in human urine
ce:section.ce:para: Pretreatment of human urine samples: Human urine samples were collected from six volunteers (three of them are normal human, and the others are hyperthyroidism patient) from the Sir Run Run Hospital of Nanjing Medical University with informed consent. Briefly, all the urine samples were first centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 20 min, and then filtered through the 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the insoluble substances. Subsequently, 2 mL of NaNO2 (600 mM) and 1 mL of H2SO4 (600 mM) were added into 1 mL of urine sample to react for 5 min (to oxidize uric I− to I2), and the excess amount of urea solution (200 mM) was dropped into the above mixing solution (to remove superfluous NaNO2) until no bubbles generated. As-produced I2 in mixing solution was extracted with CCl4 (1 mL × 3). After combining and collecting organic phases, 400 μL of Na2S2O3 (25 μM) was finally added into the CCl4 solution (to reduce I2 to I−) and re-obtained I− was further back-extracted with ultrapure water (300 μL × 2). Finally, the above 1 mL water phases were collected as I− extraction sample for the following fluorescence test paper experiments. This whole extraction/back-extraction process can be completed in 20 min.

Monitoring of I− on test paper: Each batch of the above I− extraction sample was first dropped on the as-prepared test paper, and then the color of test paper was seen under UV lamp after reacting for 3 min. Finally, the corresponding fluorescent photos were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Dispersive dDNA-AgNCs were synthesized by reducing AgNO3 with NaBH4 in the presence of the optimal dimer shaped DNA (Fig. 1 A) and the structural formation of dDNA-AgNCs by the DNA templates was shown in Fig. S2. While CDs were prepared by one step synthesis procedure (Fig. 1B). The morphologies of dDNA-AgNCs and CDs were confirmed by TEM. The TEM images show that the dDNA-AgNCs and CDs possess good monodispersity with particle sizes of ∼5 and ∼4 nm (Fig. S3A and Fig. S3C), respectively. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis gives the hydrate particle sizes of ∼12 and ∼ 6 nm for dDNA-AgNCs and CDs, respectively (Fig. S3B and Fig. S3D), which are larger than the diameters themselves because of the formation of hydration layer. This indicates that they have excellent hydrophilicity. Fig. S4 displays the FT-IR spectrum of CDs. The characteristic peak at 3373 cm−1 represents stretching vibration of O–H or N–H. The two feature peaks at 2970 cm−1 and 2895 cm−1 ascribe to stretching vibration of C–H. The adsorption bands at 1653 cm−1 and 1549 cm−1 are vibrational absorption band of CO and bending vibrations of N–H, respectively. These spectra data suggest the presence of numerous oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of CDs, which are responsible for high water solubility.

Fig. 2 shows that the fluorescence peaks at 577 nm (Fig. 2, curve a) and 446 nm (Fig. 2, curve b) correspond to the dDNA-AgNCs and CDs, respectively. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of dDNA-AgNCs is approximately 4-folds stronger than that of sDNA-AgNCs as shown in Fig. S5. When dDNA-AgNCs and CDs are simply mixed and tuned in the ratio of fluorescent intensity (orange to blue) to 5:1, the fluorescence intensity of the mixing system (Fig. 2, curve c) possess binary-emission peaks at 577 nm and 446 nm, which are nearly consistent with individual dDNA-AgNCs and CDs, respectively. When I− is added to the mixing system, the characteristic fluorescence intensity (at 577 nm) of dDNA-AgNCs is obviously quenched by I−. The fluorescence intensity (at 446 nm) of CDs keeps constant because CDs are not sensitive to I− (Fig. 2, curve d). This provides a reference signal for the ratiometric determination of I−. The corresponding orange, blue, salmon pink and light steel blue fluorescence can also be seen under a 365 nm UV lamp (the inset photo in Fig. 2), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of ratiometric fluorescent probe by iodide ion
ce:section.ce:para: For better illuminating the role of I− in the fluorescence quenching of dDNA-AgNCs, the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was first measured. As shown in Fig. S6, there is no overlap between the absorption peak of dDNA-AgNCs and that of CDs in the visible region. This phenomenon indicates that there is no energy transfer between dDNA-AgNCs and CDs. Fig. S7 shows that the absorption spectrum of I− and the emission spectrum of dDNA-AgNCs have no overlap with each other. This suggests that the fluorescence resonance energy transfer should not be the protruding mechanistic pathway. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence decay time of dDNA-AgNCs decreases from 4.77 ns to 1.85 ns when I− (9 μM) was added, that is to say, the fluorescence lifetimes before and after adding I− into the dDNA-AgNCs solution were different. Obviously, inner filter effect (IFE) is not deemed to be the quenching mechanism of the I− on dDNA-AgNCs probe, because the fluorescence lifetime should remain unchanged in IFE [17,18]. Thus, the lifetime shortening of the dDNA-AgNCs may be related to a dynamic quenching [18–20].

In order to further elucidate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, a series of experiments were carried out. The fluorescence quenching is generally divided into two parts of static quenching and dynamic quenching. They can be described by the Lineweaver−Burk equation (eq (1)) and the Stern−Volmer’s equation (eq (2)), respectively [21,22].
(1) 1 / (F0–F) = 1 / F0 + KLB / (F0·Cq)
(2) F0 / F = 1 + KSV · Cq
where F0 and F are the values of I577/I446 of the ratiometric probe in the absence and presence of quencher (I−), respectively. Cq is the concentration of the I−. KLB and KSV are the static quenching constant and the dynamic quenching constant, respectively. The plot of the relationships between 1/(F0–F) or F0/F and 1/Cq or Cq are displayed in Fig. S9A and Fig. S9B, respectively. Interestingly, at low concentration of I−, the fluorescence quenching of dDNA-AgNCs by I− does not only follow the Lineweaver–Burk equation but also Stern−Volmer’s equation, whereas at high concentration of I−, the fluorescence quenching only follows the Lineweaver–Burk equation. The results may suggest that the static and dynamic quenching seem to act together, that is to say, this is a more complex quenching process.

Based on previous studies [23–26] and our experimental results, a mechanism that I−-induced oxidative etching and aggregation lead to fluorescence quenching of dDNA-AgNCs is proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . I− as the strong nucleophilic reagent spontaneously adsorbs on the surface of dDNA-AgNCs because of the lower solubility of AgI (Ksp = 8.5 × 10−17). The pre-complexation gives rise to the disturbance on the surface of Ag atom, and further results in oxidative etching in air (O2 and H2O) and the formation of a monolayer of Agm−AgI via reaction 1 in Fig. 3. Meanwhile, the zeta-potential data (Fig. S9C) also provide the evidence of the adsorption of I− [24,25]. The chemisorbed I− functions as a bridge between AgNCs and induces the aggregating of AgNCs [27,28]. The result leads to the increase of the apparent hydrodynamic diameter (HDD) of dDNA-AgNCs (Fig. S9D). Moreover, the aggregation can bring out changes in the absorption bands of dDNA-AgNCs. As shown in Fig. S10, with increasing I− concentration, the absorbance at 483 nm (represents the characteristic absorption peak of small dDNA-AgNCs) significantly decreased and a new absorption at 417 nm (originates from the plasmon resonance absorption of the larger Ag nanoparticles resulted by the aggregation of Ag atoms) gradually appeared.

It is noted that the ways of oxidative etching and aggregation are inseparable in a known concentration range, but one of them is dominant in a certain range. As shown in Fig. 3, oxidative etching is the dominant mechanism at lower concentrations of I− (corresponding to the dynamic quenching process as mentioned above). In this process, the nanoparticles are the I−-capped Ag complex nanoparticles (Agm−AgI), rather than divided AgNCs and AgI. Meanwhile, the aggregation runs throughout the quenching process while it becomes the dominant mechanism at higher concentrations of I− (corresponding to the static quenching process as mentioned above). This is also demonstrated with the TEM images (Fig. S11). As shown in Fig. S11, the aggregation degree (particle size) of dDNA-AgNCs increases with the increasing concentration of I− and become obvious when I− is at higher concentration. The results indicate that both oxidative etching and aggregation work together in the quenching process. This is to say, at lower I− concentrations, oxidative etching is the dominant process, while at higher I− concentrations, aggregation becomes the dominant mechanism. Thus, the fluorescence quenching mechanism for the determination of I− was testified to be the I−-induced oxidative etching and aggregation of dDNA-AgNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the ratiometric fluorescent method
ce:section.ce:para: The following experimental parameters were optimized in detail: (a) the stem length of DNA strand in dDNA-AgNCs (see the detailed composition in Table S1); (b) the ratio of fluorescent intensity (orange to blue); (c) the response time of the ratiometric fluorescent probe to I−; (d) the pH value of ratiometric system and (e) the temperature of assay system. Respective data, Figs. S12–16 are given in EMS. In a word, the following test conditions were found to give optimal results: (a) 13 base pairs; (b) 5:1; (c) 1 min; (d) pH 7.4 and (e) 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe to iodide ion
ce:section.ce:para: Before the sensitivity assay, the stabilities of fluorescent probe in the absence and presence of I− were first investigated. As shown in Fig. S17, the fluorescent intensity of CDs (at 446 nm) and the fluorescent intensity ratio (I577/I446) of dDNA-AgNCs/CDs all almost kept constant, ensuring a reliable reference signal for the detection of I−.

Fig. 4 A shows the fluorescent test results by titrating different concentrations of I−. The orange fluorescence peak at 577 nm is gradually quenched, and the blue fluorescence peak at 446 nm kept steady, which are completely consistent with the cases of pure dDNA-AgNCs and CDs (Fig. S18). With the increase of I− concentration (from 0.05 to 13 μM), the fluorescence intensity ratio (I577/I446) dramatically decreases and exhibits a good linear relationship with the concentration ranging from 0.05 to 13 μM (R2 = 0.9985) (Fig. 4B). The detection limit of I− is calculated to be as low as 19.8 nM based on the basic rule of three standard deviation about the background (3σ). The spectral response to I− gradually causes consecutive color variations from salmon pink to lighter salmon pink to lilac to light steel blue to final deep sky blue under a 365 nm UV lamp (the inset photos of Fig. 4A). Such dosage-dependent color variation of binary-emissive ratiometric probe is wider and more easily distinguishable than that of single-emissive probe (dDNA-AgNCs, from bright orange to dark orange, as shown in the inset photos of Fig. S18A) in visual detection of I−.

To evaluate the selectivity of the binary-color probe to I−, changes in the fluorescence emission intensity ratio (I577/I446) in the presence of various anions were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. S19, the I577/I446 ratio is decreased about 60% by 9 μM I−, while the ratio of I577/I446 and fluorescence color have not obvious changes with the additions of 90 μM NO3 −, SCN−, F−, Cl−, Br−, IO3 −, AcO−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, S2O3 2− and PO4 3− into the probe solution, respectively (blue bars in Fig. 5 and Fig. S19A). To further verify the anti-interferences capabilities of the binary-color probe, several experiments have been carried out. As shown in red bars in Fig. 5 and Fig. S19B, the fluorescence response is not influenced even if 10-fold excesses of the interfering ion coexist with I−. Meanwhile, the simultaneous addition of 90 μM NO3 −, SCN−, F−, Cl−, Br−, IO3 −, AcO−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, S2O3 2− and PO4 3− into the probe solution, the I577/I446 ratio reduces about 14% (Fig. S20, curve b). After further addition of 9 μM I−, the I577/I446 ratio dips below 70% (Fig. S20, curve c). Although S2− perturbs the assay of I−, the addition of Zn2+ efficiently avoids its interference (Fig. S21) [29]. The results suggest that binary-color probe possesses a high selectivity to I−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual assay of iodide ion in urine using ratiometric fluorescent test paper
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to assay, we prepared the test paper independently in quintuplicate to verify the reproducibility of test paper. As shown in Fig. S22, the colors of as-prepared test papers were almost same (salmon pink), indicating that the proposed method has a well reproducibility. Moreover, the test paper was stored in the 4 °C refrigerator and kept stable in 30 days (Fig. S23). After 100 μL I− aqueous solution with different concentrations (from 0 to 13 μM) is separately dropped onto the test paper, a series of color changes can be observed under an UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 6 A, each dosage (0, 0.05, 1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13 μM) matches with a distinguishable color from salmon pink to lighter salmon pink to lilac to light steel blue to final deep sky blue, which is consistent with that in the inset photos of Fig. 4A. In particular, when I− concentration was at 0.05 μM, the test paper shows salmon pink, which is slightly lighter than that at 0 μM, but different with that at 1 μM. The discernible dosage scale as low as 0.05 μM is about 1 order of magnitude lower than the median urinary iodine concentrations (100–200 μg L−1, equal to 0.79–1.58 μM) defined by the WHO for school-age children [11]. Surprisingly, the two concentrations of I−, 1.58 and 2.36 μM (which are the upper limit of uric I− in normal human and the reference limit of uric I− in hyperthyroidism patient [11,12]), correspond to lilac and light blue, respectively (Fig. S24), indicating a very sensitive dosage-discerning capability by visualization.

Moreover, it has been reported that the concentration of Cl− in normal human urine ranges from 110 to 170 mM [13], which is about 120,000-fold higher than that of I− in normal human urine. This may interfere assay of I− in human urine. In fact, when the concentration of Cl− is larger than 10 mM (which is about 8500-fold higher than that of I− in normal human urine), the I577/I446 ratio begins to decrease (Fig. S25). To solve this problem, urine samples were pretreated by a redox and simple extraction (as described in section 2.6). As shown in Fig. 7 , the pretreatment completely eliminated the interference of Cl− by combining redox with liquid-liquid extraction.

After pretreatment, we examined the effectiveness and reliability of ratiometric fluorescent probe in the practical application for monitoring of I− in human urine. Two human urine samples were collected from the normal human (urine 1) and the hyperthyroidism patient (patient 1), and the original contents of their I− were separately measured to be 1.56 μM and 7.16 μM by ICP-MS method, indicating that uric I− level of normal human is lower than that of hyperthyroidism patient. To correspond to the linear range established in the above tests, two pretreated urine samples were diluted 10 times, yielding baseline concentrations of 0.156 μM and 0.716 μM, respectively. These diluted urine samples were then mixed with the different concentrations of I−. The values measured by the ratiometric fluorescence probe are consistent with the amounts of I− spiked into the urine samples (Fig. 6B and Table S2), the recoveries of I− are in the range of 92.18–99.61% and the relative standard deviations (RSD) are less than 6%. These confirm that the proposed method has a high accuracy to meet the practical requirement.

The same pretreated urine samples were investigated with the fluorescent test papers and observed under a 365 nm UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 6C, the colors of test papers evolve from the original salmon pink to sky blue with the increase of I− in normal human urine. Amazingly, the concentrations of I−, ∼0.1, ∼1, ∼5, ∼9 μM correspond to lighter salmon pink, lavender, light steel blue and sky blue (photo a, b, c, d in Fig. 6C), respectively, suggesting a very sensitive dosage-discernible capability through our naked eye. Similarly, different concentrations of I− in hyperthyroidism patient urine make the test paper display changes from pink-purple to lilac to light steel blue to deep sky blue (photo e, f, g, h in Fig. 6C). Obviously, the fluorescent color (lighter salmon pink, as shown in photo a of Fig. 6C) at 0.156 μM I− (equal to 1.56 μM I− in original urine of a normal human) is different from that (pink-purple, as shown in photo e of Fig. 6C) at 0.716 μM I− (equal to 7.16 μM I− in original urine of a hyperthyroidism patient). In order to further confirm the accuracy and reliability of the analytical approach, six urine samples were respectively determined by the proposed method and ICP-MS. The data are shown in Fig. S26, and it can be seen that the results by the proposed method are in good agreement with those of ICP-MS, suggesting that as-prepared test paper is actually applicable for the monitoring of I− in human urine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of this work and other methods for assay of iodide ion
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of this method is compared with those of previous works, and the results are listed in Table 1 . The detection limit of the proposed method is close to those of colorimetry [4,5,34] and spectrofluorometry [1,2,30,33]. Although ICP-MS [8] and HPLC-MS [9] have higher sensitivity than this work, they require large-scale instrument and highly trained operator, and are not fit for the on-site assay. Ion chromatography [6], electrochemistry [7], SERS [10] and two spectrofluorometric methods [31,32] also provide a slightly lower detection limit, but they can’t realize colorimetry-based visual quantification. It’s worth noting that this method displays wider color evolution with the increase of I− concentration (the inset photo of Figs. 4A and 6A), and each test is achieved within 43 min.

In this paper, a new ratiometric probe with the best visualization effect of color change has been obtained at the 5:1 ratio of orange to blue in fluorescent intensity and can be applied for quantitative analysis of pure target analyte, but difficult to achieve for trace assay of I− in unpretreated urine samples. It has been clearly demonstrated that our redox pretreatment procedure is sufficient to eliminate the strong interference caused by ultrahigh concentration of Cl− in urine. The ratiometric fluorescent test paper serves as a portable tool for specific and visual monitoring of I− in human urine, which can produce an acceptable detection limit of 50 nM and provide a preliminary judgement whether our uric I− is at a normal or abnormal level for the clinical diagnosis of hyperthyroidism. Moreover, this portable fluorescent test paper is easy to operate for a non-professional, compared with other analytical technologies. In short, the above experiments in terms of sensitivity, accuracy, reliability and practicability display excellent performance of our portable test paper for visual monitoring of I− in real human urine samples. It is believed that the portable test paper offers vast possibilities for the colorimetric and on-site sensing of other analytes in various fields, especially for healthcare.

Panpan Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Xin Xu: Data curation, Investigation. Jiangrong Ji: Data curation, Validation. Jiafeng Wu: Data curation, Software. Tian Lu: Data curation, Software. Yuhong Xia: Data curation, Software. Liping Wang: Data curation, Software. Junting Fan: Resources, Visualization. Yang Jin: Resources, Visualization. Liying Zhang: Supervision, Methodology. Shuhu Du: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775073).
